title: The women of the Bauhaus
gist: A Berlin exhibition of over 300 photographs recovers the overlooked women photographers of the Bauhaus, whose work was often unattributed, appropriated, or destroyed by political persecution, showing how institutional and market gatekeeping erased female contributions to a school nominally open to all.

themes.theme: Gender and access at the Bauhaus

insights.claim: Walter Gropius' 1919 Bauhaus manifesto announced the school was open to 'any person of good repute, without regard to age or sex,' but women who enrolled were mostly steered into 'feminine' subjects like weaving and ceramics rather than architecture or painting.
insights.speaker: Malcolm Forbes
insights.so_what: Illustrates how formally egalitarian institutional rules can still produce gendered outcomes through informal channeling.
insights.quote: any person of good repute, without regard to age or sex

insights.claim: When the Bauhaus established a dedicated photography course in 1929, nearly half of the students who enrolled for professional camera training were women, making photography one of the few technical Bauhaus disciplines where women achieved near gender parity.
insights.speaker: Malcolm Forbes
insights.so_what: Identifies photography as an outlier discipline for women's access within a male-dominated institution.
insights.locator: In 1929, when the school established a photography course

insights.claim: Despite pioneering work throughout the 1920s, most Bauhaus women photographers drifted into obscurity and are only now being recovered by a 2026 Berlin exhibition, indicating the historical art canon systematically undervalued work of comparable quality to their male peers.
insights.speaker: Malcolm Forbes
insights.so_what: Signals an ongoing institutional re-canonization trend worth tracking for cultural-value reassessment of overlooked artists.
insights.locator: many of the women who created them didn't get the recognition

insights.claim: Lucia Moholy, one of the foremost female architectural photographers of her era, fled Berlin in 1933 and had to leave her negatives behind; Walter Gropius later published her uncredited images to promote the Bauhaus's architecture, and it took until 1957, after a prolonged legal dispute, for the negatives to be returned to her.
insights.speaker: Malcolm Forbes
insights.so_what: A concrete case of institutional appropriation of a woman creator's work without credit, resolved only through decades of legal action.
insights.quote: It wasn't until 1957 that Moholy's negatives were returned to her, following a prolonged legal dispute.

insights.claim: Elsa Thiemann and Etel Mittag-Fodor sold photojournalism to photo agencies during the 1920s-30s boom, but their images were published anonymously or under the agency's name, making it almost impossible for them to build individual professional reputations.
insights.speaker: Malcolm Forbes
insights.so_what: An early example of how distribution intermediaries can strip creator attribution and career capital from originators.

insights.claim: Irena Blühová, who photographed social injustice for the leftist newspaper Arbeiter-Illustrierte-Zeitung, went underground in the Czechoslovak antifascist resistance in 1942 and had all her negatives destroyed; she spent the postwar years reconstructing her body of work from previously published prints.
insights.speaker: Malcolm Forbes
insights.so_what: Shows political persecution, not just market neglect, as a distinct mechanism erasing women's Bauhaus-era archives.

insights.claim: The Museum of Photography Berlin's exhibition 'New Woman, New Vision: Women Photographers of the Bauhaus' assembles over 300 photographs and runs until 4 October, framed by the reviewer as an overdue reassessment of the school's female artists.
insights.speaker: Malcolm Forbes
insights.so_what: A data point on the broader institutional trend of re-canonizing overlooked 20th-century women artists in major European museums.
insights.locator: New Woman, New Vision: Women Photographers of the Bauhaus is at the Museum of Photography

insights.claim: Paul Klee, Wassily Kandinsky and László Moholy-Nagy dominate the standard historical record of Bauhaus teaching staff, illustrating how canon formation around 'great male masters' persisted even at an institution founded on formally egalitarian admission.
insights.speaker: Malcolm Forbes
insights.so_what: Explains why recovering women's contributions requires deliberate curatorial intervention decades later.
insights.locator: Much has been written about the male artists who lectured there
